speaker: interviewer
text: The date is the 17th of March, 1995. Survivor's name is Jacob Gang. Interviewer is Brenda Halperin. The city and state is Toronto, in Ontario in the country of Canada, and the interview is conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is March 17, 1995. We're interviewing Mr. Jacob Gang, known as Janek Sim throughout the war. I am Brenda Halperin, and we're interviewing in Toronto, Canada. Jacob, will you tell us a little bit about yourself? Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Sasów, Poland, which is known as Galicia. I was born in 1932.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- the town of Sasów?

speaker: interviewee
text: The town of Sasów is a small Jewish town. A lot-- a lot of religious people used to live there, like before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- you grew up with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I grew up, you know, in-- in that town. My parents were very religious. We used to go to haida. We had a lot of fun when we were kids, but it didn't last very long.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had-- who was in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: My house was my father, my mother, and my two year older brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Simcha. He was two years older than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell us a little bit about the house you grew up in?

speaker: interviewee
text: The house? The house was a Jewish, typical, you know, religious house. Every Shabbos, you know, we used to go to shul. Every Friday afternoon, most of the businesses in town were closed. We used to go-- there was a-- like a steam bath in that town, with a mikvah. The women used to go there, like the men used to go there. Every Friday I used to go with my father, about 2, 3 o'clock. And then we used to get dressed for shul. And it was very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, I would say, was a peddler.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after Shabbos, did your mother go on Shabbos as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Friday-- Friday night, the women didn't go, but the women used to go Shabbos. And then I used to go with my father on Shabbos, and after shul we used to come home. Everything was like prepared, you know, like real, real, you know Yiddish, you know meals which [INAUDIBLE] everything. And then the kids used to go to play, and the parents used to stay home. We used to play all afternoon, like we used to go to the woods, here, there. We had a good time, but it didn't last very long. In-- in 1939, the Russians came in. It was still OK until 1941, when the Germans came in. Our childhood was finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to while you were still growing up. You say you enjoyed your-- you had a very warm childhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to what kind of a school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a public school. And I also went to--

speaker: interviewer
text: A Polish public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a Polish public school. They used to teach Polish and Ukrainian, because-- And also, I used to go to haida. Matter of fact, the rabbi used to come to my house to teach.

speaker: interviewer
text: To teach you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- when you went to school, did you encounter any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we encountered a lot. The-- the Polish and Ukrainian kids, we used to fight almost every day. They used to call us [POLISH] you know, and all kinds of-- they used to throw stones at us. But we-- we-- we fought them off. It wasn't such, like-- you know, killings, until the Germans came in. Then, the killings started.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to school as a group? With a group?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We met in school, because, you know, on my street I think there was about two or three kids, on my street, and there were others. We used to meet in school and play in school. We had a good time in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about the teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: The teachers? The teachers were OK. We didn't have too much of a problem with teachers, but it-- it were the kids by itself. Like, before school and after school, and recess. But we-- we used to fight them a little bit. But it wasn't like to-- to the death, you know. We used to fight with stones, with sticks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and at home, you said that Shabbos-- did you have visitors for Shabbos? Did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes. Matter of fact, we had-- I wouldn't say every Saturday. I remember when we used to have our own house, and my mother baked. I think it was every two weeks. We had, you know, an oven. We used to bake bread, challah, and cake. And it came like Friday morning, like when we got up, I see all these cakes that my mother used to put away-- cakes, and the bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this-- I used to ask her, you know, what is the food? My mother said, [YIDDISH]. And to this day, I don't know. They used to-- there were a few-- Do you want to translate what she said, for those who only speak English? You said it in Yiddish, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: [YIDDISH] [? These ?] [? are. ?] But there was a few-- a few poor people. They used to, like, make Shabbos for them. So the butcher, the-- you know, the groceries, they used to collect for these people in town. And my mother was one of them, but she wouldn't tell me. To this day, I don't know for who or what.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which, I-- I believe it's right. It's better we didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right. And-- and first, you said you were under the Russians? The Russians came into Sasow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when the Russians came in, it was OK. We didn't have too much of a problem. Went to school, we learned-- we-- we were taught Russian. No problem at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you spoke Ukrainian, Polish--

speaker: interviewee
text: I spoke Polish, Ukrainian, and Russian at the time-- and Yiddish-- at the time. And then when the Germans came in, the Russians left. It was just the Ukrainians and the Poles. They gave us a very, very hard time. A very hard time.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this, that the Germans came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: June, '41. Soon as the war started, I think the next day they were there. They came in like a-- like-- marched in like to a parade. And as soon as they marched in, I remember the Russians were there at ten o'clock in the morning, and they asked anybody that wants to leave, they were going to take with. At 11 o'clock the Germans came in. By two o'clock, they surrounded already all the elite in my town. They took them out with these wagons into the woods, and they shot about a half the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say the elite, who-- who are you referring to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm referring to doctors and the Jewish leaders. You know, the-- a little bit, you know, more educated. Teachers. There was one like a drugstore, I remember. There was a doctor. There were a few businesspeople. They took them all out. Matter of fact, my-- one of my cousins, too. He used to play in weddings, him and his son. They didn't know from no Communists, no politics, but the Gentiles used to point out when they came in, you the Communist, you the Communist. They didn't know from nothing. They took them all out and shot them, about half the town. That was the first afternoon when they came in. And then it started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know the people who pointed out all the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was mostly the, you know, the young kids, the Gentiles, you know. It's very hard for me to point out right now. But when I was there, like, I remember. But right now, it's-- it's too far. I mean, it's hard to remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And then? After that, then--

speaker: interviewee
text: After that-- I think it wasn't very long-- they proclaimed like all the Jews should wear the yellow stars. I never wore the yellow star, but my parents did. And after a while-- I think it was the next--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it because you were young that you didn't wear the yellow star, or you just refused?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just-- I was a little bit mischievous those days. I wasn't afraid of anything, OK? So I-- I used to go around, you know, amongst them. Matter of fact, we used to play-- when the Russians left, they used to-- they left, you know, tanks and ammunition, everything. We used to play with all this here. We-- we weren't afraid of anything. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And then, they took us. I think it was-- they came in June-- the next winter, they took us to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is the winter of '42? Or was it the '41?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was in '41, the winter, yeah. Like the most of us, my two year older brother, they right away they made raids. And they took most of the young people to the labor camps. They were about a-- a mile from us. They took my two year older brother. They took my oldest brother to the camp. I was too young.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and your brothers' names were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Simcha. He was two years older than me. And Abi was my half-brother. He was the oldest. Those two were in the labor camp in Sasow, not far. And us they took to-- to the ghettos, Zolochiv.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who-- who did they take to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took me, my mother, and my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Golda, Leah Golda.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her maiden name was Sandel. That-- that's the name-- when I escaped from the ghetto, that's the name I used right through the war, Sandel. Instead of Janek-- Jacob-- I was called Janek. That was it, till-- till-- till right after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they take you to-- from Sasow to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Gentiles took us with sleds. It was in wintertime, very cold. We almost froze to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember being-- how that happened? Were you-- were you told in the house, or was there an announcement?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Was an announcement. Was an announcement, this and this day, and everybody had to move. And they took-- took about, I don't know, a few hours, and we came to Zolochiv. At Zolochiv, they gave us one little room, a cubicle. And that was-- that was it. It was like a concentration camp. It was-- then they-- they put up the barbed like the wires, you know, like closing off the streets. You couldn't get out. But I lived right by the-- my house, where I stayed in the ghetto--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a house or an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an apartment. It was right near the wires, by the fence, so I used to stay there and watch. And I used to go out every day-- used to go to the villages, for food. If I didn't bring food, we-- we would have starved. People used to die like flies in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to-- there was a Judenrat. There was a Jewish committee in the ghetto, which they gave a little bit of milk and a little bit of food. Very little, very little. You couldn't live on it. And most people died. There was-- there was typhoid and sicknesses, don't ask. So I used to go out, and I-- I got caught once.

speaker: interviewer
text: And?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got caught inside, when I came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had a-- a sack of potatoes, and flour, and bread. It was a Jewish policeman. He caught me inside, already in the ghetto, and he took my sack and threw it all on the ground. And the kids and the people, you know, when he caught me, they grabbed everything. And he hit me with the-- those-- they had those rubber sticks. I can still feel it. He could have let me go. He didn't have to hit me. But anyway, I can still feel it. But then the next day I-- I did the same thing again, because there was nothing else. Otherwise, if you sit, you just starved to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in '42, so about 10 years old or so. 10, 11, something like that. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeah, 11, or maybe 12. Something like this, I don't remember exactly the date.

speaker: interviewee
text: Around there. We stayed in the ghetto, I think it was that winter, and then the-- the next winter. My mother, I remember, took me up to one of the houses there where the old people used to congregate and [INAUDIBLE]. I think that was my bar mitzvah there-- they-- they made a prayer. And after that, they liquidated the ghetto. They took everybody out. And my mother told me to hide, so I hid. You're-- you're talking about just your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My father died already in the ghetto, the first-- the first winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: The first winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- did he-- he die of disease?

speaker: interviewee
text: Typhoid. Yeah, typhoid.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now you say-- were there other boys who went out with you, or did you do this all on your own?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. From the ghetto, I went out on my own. When we stayed like in Sasow, we used to go to the village with other boys, too. But I remember from Sasow I went to one of the villages. On the way back, one of the German high command-- like he was in charge of all the camp there-- and he stopped and he wanted to give us a lift. The other-- the other two boys went in. He gave them a lift. When I came to town, they were all gone. They made a raid. They took all the boys, like just-- just the kids. They-- they used to take them into the school-- well, they used the-- like, they took the blood for the German soldiers. Most of them died on the tables. I was lucky. I didn't-- I didn't want a lift. I didn't want to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this when you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was in Sasow, before the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's when you were still in Sasow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. From the ghetto, I used to go out alone. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because you know, I knew other kids there, too. They used to go here and there, like surroundings. But I didn't know them that well. And their mothers maybe wouldn't let them out. My mother was crying every time I went out, but she was glad to see me when I came back. See, outside, I mean, nobody-- I didn't look Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you fair?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was like a dark brown, like blondish a little bit, I didn't look Jewish at all. And I spoke perfect Ukrainian and Polish. So if-- if I met like Ukrainians, I was Ukrainian. I met the Polish, I'm Polish. That was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and you say that after about the second winter--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after they liquidated the ghetto--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I ran away from the ghetto, I had nothing on, so I tried to go back you know, like inside and take some clothes. But I was standing outside you know, by the wires, with the rest of the people-- you know, like civilians. They were watching. One of the Ukrainian policeman came over to me and says, well, you know, what are you doing here? So I told him in Ukrainian, nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he says to me in Ukrainian, if I catch you once more, I'll-- I'll-- I'll shoot you like a Jew. But he said it in Ukrainian. So when he said it. Like a Jew, I-- you know, I-- I was already a little lighter. I was happier when he said, like a Jew. And-- and when the-- when it was liquidated, the ghetto was liquidated--

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was liquidated, that was it. Then I went to the camp where my older brother was there. I used to sneak in there at night to sleep, or I used to sneak into the barracks to sleep, because it was very cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and the-- the other men didn't mind that you came in to sleep there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- a lot of them, they were from Sasow. I mean, they knew me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you came there to sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went in a few times there. Not-- not too many times.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was-- which brother was this that you were with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two year older brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was in the labor camp, but I used to sneak in--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was-- was this Simcha?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Before they went to work, like early morning, I used to sneak out before. Then I used to go to the villages. They caught me once under a haystack. I still have-- I have very long wounds here from bayonets, when they caught me, the Ukrainian police with the German Gestapos. They caught me. They caught another couple from my town. And then they caught another couple from Zloczów, older people. They took us all into Zloczów again, into the ghetto, and they told us to undress. I was tied up. I didn't have a belt, but I had a string. And we looked at each other. We knew already, because I used to see them, what they used to do. Every time they-- they took-- they caught the Jews, they put them against the wall. They made them undress, lie down. And they had machine guns, and they shot them right in the back, you know, the lung or whatever. They went like this to each one, naked.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we knew already what's going on. We looked at each other. We took one look, and we started to run. So it was like-- see that square on the wall-- so I just snuck out. I heard the-- you know, the machine gun, the bullets whistle. I ran into the house, and I hid there till-- it was very dark, maybe 12, 1 o'clock at night. It was very dark. Did they come after you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't go into the building, but I hid there. It was very dark. I-- I went out and went back to the villages.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you-- this-- was this before you undressed, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, after. Before. No. We-- we started to run before-- before we undressed. Like I-- I started to play around, you know, with the knot, with the string. Instead of opening up the belt to get undressed, I started to play around. We took one look at each other, and we just started to run.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the-- were the Germans who were watching-- was it Germans who were watching you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was two Ukrainian policemen and one German SS. Like he was--

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they distracted by something, that-- or you just--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were able to just escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's like you can't-- you haven't got time to think. You like-- you give each other the eye. But before you give each other the eye already, we started to run. That was it. I've never seen the others again.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't come into the building with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no, no. We started to run like in different directions. I was right by the corner, so I ran in-- you know, beside the corner, and the first door, I ran into the building and right up-- upstairs. We were-- we were scared to death, because we knew. We knew. I mean, we weren't afraid already like, you know, to die, but we knew already. We saw it. I was caught a few times, but each time I ran away.

speaker: interviewer
text: This time, you went from there to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to the villages.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do in the villages?

speaker: interviewee
text: Used to beg, I used to sneak into the barracks to sleep. In the summertime I used to work for them, like help out in the fields. And I just waiting for something. We didn't know what. You know, maybe the older people knew, but about liberation, or the Russians, or this-- I had no idea. Nothing. But then, when I-- when-- when we were in the villages, some Russian partisans came-- came in. Like they were prisoners of war that worked also in labor camps, and they ran away. So I joined those what they-- like, the partisans, and I was with them I would say at least six, seven months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you stay when you were with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in the woods. We stayed most of the time in the woods. We used to go in at night, you know, to the people. They gave us food. But then the Germans used to hunt us. But they never went into the woods, never. The only ones that went into the woods were the Ukrainian police. They sent them in. The Germans themselves never went in. And there were woods for miles and miles and miles. You can go in there, disappear forever. Anyway, with these woods, we-- we started to go like east and we wound up closer. The-- the Russians were coming closer, and we wound up by [POLISH]. We met with the Russian army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before-- before you talk about being with the Russian army, let's go-- go back to when you're with the partisans. How-- approximately how many were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say there were at least 50, about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a leader?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one. There was-- there was one leader. They called him capitan.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name? I think was Bialov. There were-- there were so many, it's very hard to remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you make any plans to attack as a group? A partisan group? Did you have weapons?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have any weapons at the time, but-- but when-- as we went like, north, like, through every village, when we used to-- before we used to go in, I used to go in and check out to see if, you know, if there's German army, the police, and how many is there, what-- if we can stay for a while. You know, near coming for food. Because sometimes they had German-- the army concentration, like for the army. We wouldn't go in there with too many. But if there was no army stationed there, just the Ukrainian police, we used to go in there at night for food and shelter, whatever we can get. And then we-- then we-- they-- they-- they got a few guns, you know, from the Germans, like surprises-- like small arms.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they didn't really plan any battles themselves as a group?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't-- we didn't have-- with what? No, that's-- obviously. But later on-- later on, when before the end, we had a few guns already. And we used to, like, on-- on-- on the way-- like, if there were just, let's say, one German wagon, or two, we used to attack them. Like take whatever they had and kill the-- the Germans. But we didn't-- we couldn't attack, let's say it was a whole company, battalion. There was no way. We didn't have the-- the power to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- were you as-- with this group as a Jew, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Not as a Jew. No, nobody knew I was Jewish. Even the villages, or the group-- nobody knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there other Jews in the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the-- in the partisan group-- sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. They were just soldiers from the Russian army that escaped the labor camps. There were no Jews there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were with them for approximately--

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say at least six, seven months. And then when we crossed, they took everybody into the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you crossed what?

speaker: interviewee
text: By [POLISH], when we met with the Russian army. That was in [POLISH]. It's not far from Dubno, about 40 kilometers. So they took everybody into the army. I was too young. They didn't want to take me. But the-- the-- the commander and the guys says, he used to help us a lot. I think you should-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] I think-- I think you should take him. Anyways, they took me. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we marched from 4 o'clock in the morning from [POLISH] to Dubno. It was such a snowstorm, you wouldn't believe it. The-- even the horses couldn't get through with the sleighs. When we came to Dubno, they took everybody's name. So they asked me where I was born.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time it was Poland, so they sent me into the Polish army. I didn't want to go to the Polish army. I wanted to go to the Russian army. But it didn't matter to me, as long as-- as long-- as long as I went in, because all I wanted was revenge. That's it. And from Dubno they-- they sent us to Sumy. It was about 300 kilometers east of Kiev.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the Russian army?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the-- no no, that was-- that was the Polish army. Like they sent us into Sumy with Russia. That's when the Polish army formed, in 1942. I think it was [INAUDIBLE] General Berling's army. Because the Polish army, that was Anders' army, they were Russia's. They didn't want to go to fight with the Germans. They went to the Middle East.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I think we'll continue right there in the next tape.
